blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: In this chapter, I have collected together the folklore in the animals and plants mentioned in this book and arranged them alphabetically. Of course there are many other animate and inanimate things round which similar traditions have grouped themselves, but I feel it advisable to confine my remarks only to those which are required for studying these pages.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Antelope. The gazelle's skin is much in use among ascetics. Shiva is supposed to wear it. In some parts of India antelopes are not killed by Hindus. The Saint Shring Rishi was born of a nymph transformed into a doe.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bear (p. 63). Jāmbuvant was the king of bears, or perhaps of a primitive tribe wearing the mask of a bear. His daughter Jāmbuvanti married Krishna (see Ganesh for folklore).

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Boar. Varāh. In his third incarnation Vishnu was a boar.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bull. Nandi (pp. 56 & 87) is the charger of Shiva. It is worshipped along with that God, or separately as Basāppa by Lingāyats in the Kanara District.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: W.
